title: Ramesh Kumar Sharma vs. Hindustan Technocraft Pvt. Ltd. & Anr.
keyArguments: The termination of the petitioner was effected without following the principles of natural justice, as no show cause notice was issued prior to dismissal.
The respondent company failed to conduct a proper domestic inquiry as mandated under the Standing Orders applicable to the establishment.
The petitioner had an unblemished service record of fourteen years and the alleged misconduct was neither proven nor substantiated with documentary evidence.
The termination order was arbitrary and violative of Section 25-F of the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947, as retrenchment compensation was not paid.
The Labour Court erred in dismissing the reference without appreciating that the burden of proof lay squarely on the employer to justify the termination.
judgmentSummary: The Delhi High Court set aside the order of the Labour Court and held that the termination of the petitioner-workman was illegal and unjustified. The Court found that the respondent employer had violated principles of natural justice by dismissing the employee without issuing a proper show cause notice or conducting a fair domestic inquiry. The petitioner was directed to be reinstated with full back wages and continuity of service.
judgmentText:
IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI
W.P.(C) No. 7834 of 2022

RAMESH KUMAR SHARMA ... Petitioner
Versus
HINDUSTAN TECHNOCRAFT PVT. LTD. & ANR. ... Respondents

Date of Decision: 14th March, 2023

CORAM: HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SUNIL PRAKASH VERMA

JUDGMENT

1. The present writ petition has been filed under Article 226 of the Constitution of India challenging the award dated 09.08.2022 passed by the Presiding Officer, Labour Court-VII, Karkardooma Courts, Delhi, whereby the reference made by the appropriate government under Section 10 of the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947 was decided against the petitioner-workman.

2. The petitioner was employed as a Senior Technician with Respondent No.1 since January 2008. On 15.03.2021, he was served with a termination letter alleging habitual absenteeism and insubordination. The petitioner contends that no show cause notice was ever issued to him, no domestic inquiry was held, and he was never afforded an opportunity to be heard before the impugned order of termination was passed.

3. Learned counsel for the petitioner, Mr. Anil Dawar, strenuously argued that the termination was effected in gross violation of the principles of natural justice and the provisions of the Industrial Employment (Standing Orders) Act, 1946. He submitted that the Labour Court committed a manifest error in placing the burden of proof on the workman rather than on the employer. He further urged that the petitioner had served the respondent company for over fourteen years without any adverse entry in his service record.

4. Per contra, learned counsel for the respondent, Ms. Priya Sehgal, submitted that the petitioner was a habitual absentee and that his services were validly terminated in accordance with the terms of employment. She argued that the Labour Court had correctly appreciated the evidence on record.

5. I have heard the learned counsels at length and perused the record carefully.

6. The settled legal position, as enunciated by the Supreme Court in Workmen of Firestone Tyre & Rubber Co. v. Management (1973) 1 SCC 813 and reaffirmed in numerous subsequent decisions, is that where an employer seeks to justify the dismissal of a workman on the ground of misconduct, the burden lies entirely on the employer to prove such misconduct through a properly conducted domestic inquiry. In the present case, the respondent has admittedly not conducted any inquiry whatsoever. The termination letter is bereft of any particulars and amounts to no more than a bald allegation.

7. Furthermore, the respondent failed to comply with Section 25-F of the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947, inasmuch as neither one month's notice nor retrenchment compensation equivalent to fifteen days' wages for each completed year of service was tendered to the petitioner at the time of termination. This Court finds that the Labour Court failed to address this statutory infirmity.

8. It is well established that an order of termination passed without affording the employee an opportunity of hearing is void ab initio. The respondent's conduct in summarily dismissing a long-serving employee of fourteen years standing, without so much as a preliminary inquiry, reflects an arbitrary exercise of power that cannot receive the imprimatur of this Court.

9. In view of the foregoing, the impugned award dated 09.08.2022 is hereby set aside. The termination of the petitioner is declared illegal and void. The respondent is directed to reinstate the petitioner in service forthwith with full back wages and continuity of service from the date of termination. The respondent shall also pay costs of Rs. 25,000/- to the petitioner.

10. The writ petition is accordingly allowed.

(SUNIL PRAKASH VERMA)
JUDGE
MARCH 14, 2023
